blocks.text: A prayer of the prophet Habakkuk according to Shigionoth.

1 : The book's second superscription (see also 1.1) not only marks this chapter as a but also provides instruction for its musical performance. The singular form of appears in the superscription to Ps 7; its meaning is unknown. Other musical notations end vv. 3,9,13, and the book as a whole. They may indicate that, like the Psalms, Hab 3 had its origins in Temple liturgy.
O Lord, I have heard of your renown, and I stand in awe, O Lord, of your work. In our own time revive it; in our own time make it known; in wrath may you remember mercy.
2 : The prophet makes his opening petition for God to God's saving work in and show .
God came from Teman, the Holy One from Mount Paran. Selah His glory covered the heavens, and the earth was full of his praise.

3 : As in Deut 33.2 and Judg 5.4, the divine warrior began his march from (synonymous with Edom, as in Ob 9) and , areas in Transjordan associated with God's dramatic deliverance of the Israelites from Egypt. , as in vv. 9 and 13, is a notation common to Psalms; its meaning is unclear, although it may indicate a musical pause.
The brightness was like the sun; rays came forth from his hand, where his power lay hidden.
Before him went pestilence, and plague followed close behind.
5 : and are pictured as divine attendants; cf. Ps 79.49. (Heb “Resheph”) is a well-attested deity in nonbiblical sources.
He stopped and shook the earth; he looked and made the nations tremble. The eternal mountains were shattered; along his ancient pathways the everlasting hills sank low.
I saw the tents of Cushan under affliction; the tent-curtains of the land of Midian trembled.
7 : Although the location of is unknown, it is here linked with , another area connected with the Transjordanian wanderings of the Israelites.
Was your wrath against the rivers, O Lord? Or your anger against the rivers,a or your rage against the sea, when you drove your horses, your chariots to victory?
You brandished your naked bow, sated were the arrows at your command. Selah You split the earth with rivers.
The mountains saw you, and writhed; a torrent of water swept by; the deep gave forth its voice. The sun raised high its hands;
the moon stood still in its exalted place, at the light of your arrows speeding by, at the gleam of your flashing spear.

4–11 : The description of God's dominance over , , etc., has strong mythical connotations and may allude to the cosmologies of other ancient Near Eastern cultures, in which and were deities defeated by a more powerful god (see translation notes and ). Parallels are found in Pss 74.12–15; 89.9–10. Throughout these verses, the divine warrior's scope is cosmic, as God brandishes , and while subduing all the earth.
10–11 : These and other verses are difficult to translate. , a common image in depictions of theophanies (divine appearances); cf. Pss 29.5–8; 114.3–7.
In fury you trod the earth, in anger you trampled nations.
12 : As in Nah 1, God's is understood to be a positive force for action against injustice.
You came forth to save your people, to save your anointed. You crushed the head of the wicked house, laying it bare from foundation to roof.d Selah
You pierced with their own arrows the head of his warriors, who came like a whirlwind to scatter us, gloating as if ready to devour the poor who were in hiding.
You trampled the sea with your horses, churning the mighty waters.

3–15 : A vision of the divine warrior. Although in Hebrew the verb tenses are ambiguous, the NRSV has rendered them in the past tense to underscore that the vision is a memory of how God acted in the past, particularly in delivering the Israelites from Egyptian bondage.
15 : Further echoes of God's deliverance of the Israelites at the Red Sea (see Ex 15).

I hear, and I tremble within; my lips quiver at the sound. Rottenness enters into my bones, and my steps tremble beneath me. I wait quietly for the day of calamity to come upon the people who attack us.

16 : Having heard a recital of the divine warrior's power in vv. 3–15, the prophet now is awestruck and is able to trust that God will indeed act again. He has received the vision promised by God in 2.3 and can (2.20; see also 2.4n) for the for his enemies. Since the liturgy to which the prophet is portrayed as responding is likely postexilic, the chapter as a whole and perhaps the book itself may date to the postexilic period.

Though the fig tree does not blossom, and no fruit is on the vines; though the produce of the olive fails, and the fields yield no food; though the flock is cut off from the fold, and there is no herd in the stalls,
yet I will rejoice in the Lord; I will exult in the God of my salvation.
God, the Lord, is my strength; he makes my feet like the feet of a deer, and makes me tread upon the heights. To the leader: with stringed instruments.

3.1–19: The prayer of the prophet. The prophet, unsatisfied with God's previous responses, turns from complaint to petition. He invokes the biblical memory that God once marched as a divine warrior to vindicate the people (Nah 1; Ex 15; Deut 33; Isa 13) as the basis for his request that God march again to establish justice.
16–19a : The prophet's response to the vision.
17–19 : Although God's saving acts are not yet evident and the people and its land continue to suffer, the vision has allowed the prophet to trust in God's saving power.

19 : The book closes with a psalmlike affirmation that God is the prophet's (Ps 118.14) and makes his feet (Ps 18.33 = 2 Sam 22.34). The concluding instruction to the music to accompany the text with (see Pss 4; 6; 45; 54; 55; etc.) reinforces the psalmlike style of the chapter.
